title: Fibonacci Sequence Generator with Incorrect Handling of Edge Cases

statement.markdown: Implement a function to generate the Fibonacci sequence up to the nth term. However, there is a bug in the function that causes incorrect results for edge cases (n=0 or n=1).
statement.inputFormat: Input will be a single integer n, representing the number of terms in the sequence.
statement.outputFormat: Output should be a string of integers, separated by spaces, representing the Fibonacci sequence.
statement.notes: You may assume that the input will always be a non-negative integer.

constraints.description: Number of terms in the sequence

hints.text: First hint text: Check the edge cases for n=0 and n=1

editorial.title: Editorial
editorial.intuition: The Fibonacci sequence is a series of numbers where a number is found by adding up the two numbers before it. Starting with 0 and 1, and then 1 and 1, and so on.
editorial.bruteForce: One way to generate the Fibonacci sequence is to use a brute force approach, where we calculate each term recursively.
editorial.betterSolution: However, a more efficient way to generate the Fibonacci sequence is to use a loop, where we store the last two numbers in variables and update them in each iteration.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution is to use a loop to store the last two numbers in variables, and update them in each iteration, which has a time complexity of O(n).
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the solution can be proved by induction.
editorial.dryRun: Step-by-step example run: For n=5, the sequence is 0, 1, 1, 2, 3.
commonMistakes: Mistake 1: Forgetting to handle edge cases
Mistake 2: Using a recursive approach instead of a loop

starterCode.cpp: class Solution {public: std::string generateFibonacci(int n) { // TODO: implement } };
starterCode.java: public class Solution { public String generateFibonacci(int n) { // TODO: implement } }

buggyStarterCode.cpp: class Solution {public: std::string generateFibonacci(int n) { if (n <= 0) return "0"; std::string result = "1"; if (n == 1) return result; for (int i = 2; i <= n; i++) { result += " " + (i + 1); } return result; }};
buggyStarterCode.java: public class Solution { public String generateFibonacci(int n) { if (n <= 0) return "0"; String result = "1"; if (n == 1) return result; for (int i = 2; i <= n; i++) { result += " " + (i + 1); } return result; }};
